hook.text: Endometriosis can start younger than you think needing to use Here is what parents should know
hook.emotional_mechanism_raw: Surprise and concern — challenges the belief that endometriosis only affects adults, creating worry for parents about their children's health.
hook.target_audience_raw: Parents, teachers, classmates, and the general public; also those already diagnosed with endometriosis.

main_claim.text: Endometriosis can start during adolescence (as early as 11-12 years old), not only at ages 18-21, and it severely impacts education, social life, and mental health, yet symptoms like severe pain or passing out are often dismissed as 'normal' periods.

supporting_explanation.summary: The speaker urges parents and teachers to recognize warning signs such as needing strong painkillers or fainting on day one of the period, and highlights the long-term consequences on education and social development, as well as the stigma girls face.

funnel_rationale: The video educates about early symptoms and impacts of endometriosis, targeting those who may be recognizing symptoms or seeking diagnosis/treatment; it also indirectly promotes a specialist nurse consultation.

cta.wording: Go to the link in our bio to book a consultation with Liz Bruen.
cta.requested_action_raw: Click the link in the bio to schedule a consultation with endometriosis nurse Liz Bruen.
cta.value_exchange_raw: Access to a private virtual consultation with the first endometriosis nurse in Wales, now in private practice.

topic.primary_raw: Early onset of endometriosis in adolescents
secondary_raw: Impact of endometriosis on education and social life, need for awareness in schools, available specialist care

speaker.type_raw: Endometriosis nurse specialist, former first endometriosis nurse in Wales

format_raw: Video (35 seconds) – talking head with onscreen text overlay
